mnemonics.title: OVERFITTING PREVENTION
mnemonics.content: DROP THE NOISE: Dropout, Regularization, Over-sampling, Pruning, Hyperparameter tuning, Noise handling, Optimization, Ensemble methods, Sampling techniques

mnemonics.title: DATA QUALITY ISSUES
mnemonics.content: MISSNICH: Missing values, Inconsistent formats, Noisy data, Class imbalance, Non-standardized data, Incomplete records, Correlated features, High dimensionality

mnemonics.title: ML CHALLENGES CATEGORIES
mnemonics.content: DACE: Data challenges, Algorithmic challenges, Computational challenges, Ethical challenges

acronyms.usage: Creating systems that learn from data without explicit programming
Broader field of creating intelligent machines
Neural networks with multiple layers for learning representations
EU regulation affecting how ML models use personal data
Adds absolute value of coefficients as penalty term
Adds squared value of coefficients as penalty term

visualTips.visualization: Imagine shooting arrows at a target. High bias means arrows consistently miss the bullseye in the same direction (systematic error). High variance means arrows scatter everywhere (sensitive to noise). Optimal is tight cluster around bullseye.
Draw a simple straight line through scattered points (underfitting), an overly wiggly line touching every point (overfitting), and a smooth curve capturing the trend (good fit).
Imagine sparse data points in 3D space. As dimensions increase to 100+, points become like tiny islands in an ocean of empty space, making distance calculations meaningless.
Picture a dirty water filter. Raw data is muddy water; cleaning it removes impurities (noise), filling holes (missing values), and removing debris (outliers) to get clean water (quality data).

analogies.analogy: Like a student who memorizes textbook answers but fails when given new questions. The student (model) learned training examples perfectly but cannot apply knowledge to new situations.
Like a chef selecting and preparing ingredients. Raw data is like unprocessed vegetables; feature engineering is chopping, marinating, and combining them into meaningful ingredients for the recipe.

Like choosing between a GPS that always points slightly east (bias) but is consistent, versus one that randomly points anywhere within 10km (variance). You want one that's both accurate on average and consistent.
Like a mysterious fortune teller who gives predictions but cannot explain the reasoning. You get answers but no understanding of how they were derived.
Like training a face recognition system only on one ethnicity's photos and then expecting it to recognize everyone equally well. The model inherits the skewed perspective from training data.
